A video circulating online shows a child - Abhinav Arora - being introduced as a âspiritual leaderâ in a school-classroom setting, portraying him as a sort of guru in front of peers. The footage has sparked intense backlash across social media, as critics question the appropriateness and ethics of presenting a minor as a spiritual authority in an academic environment. Many viewers find the spectacle unsettling - arguing that childhood is being overshadowed by an unwarranted mantle of reverence.

Among the criticism is concern over whether the child truly understands the responsibilities thrust upon him, or is instead being used as a tool for optics and social media attention. Some have flagged that such public veneration of a minor - especially in a place meant for education - risks confusing children about boundaries between schooling and religious leadership. Others see it as exploitation: a childâs right to a normal childhood, to learn and grow without undue pressure, being sacrificed at the altar of spectacle.

At the same time, supporters of the video argue that the child is simply sharing spiritual teachings and that his presence inspires devotion among believers. 
This incident raises urgent questions about responsibility - of parents, school authorities, and society at large - when involving children in matters of public faith and reverence. As the video travels further across platforms, the spotlight is no longer just on one boy - but on what kind of childhood and what kind of respect we are endorsing when religion, education, and social media converge.

In ancient Roman and later Renaissance weddings, the children we now call flower girls didnât carry pretty flowers at all. Instead, young girls walked in front of the bride carrying bundles of garlic, herbs, and wheat. At the time, weddings were believed to attract spiritsâboth good and badâso the garlic and strong-smelling herbs were thought to drive away evil forces that might curse the marriage. The wheat represented fertility, prosperity, and the hope that the couple would have many healthy children.

So the sweet modern image of a little girl tossing rose petals actually started as a serious protective ritual, where children acted like miniature guardians, warding off supernatural trouble while blessing the future family.

Spain just made history .. it became the first country in Europe to approve paid menstrual leave for women dealing with severe period pain.

And honestly?
Itâs about time a government admitted what women live with every month â
cramps that make you dizzy, pain that knocks you out, and a world that still tells you to âpush through it.â

This law means women with conditions like endometriosis or disabling cramps can finally take paid time off without being made to feel dramatic or weak.

But hereâs the part people donât talk about:
Even though the law existsâ¦ many women are still scared to use it.
Because judgment, stigma, and workplace silence are still very real.

A win on paper isnât the same as a win in real life â
but this is a step forward for all of us.

One country finally said our pain is valid.
Now the rest of the world needs to catch up.

Source: Spanish Parliament (2023), AP News, Euronews, The Guardian.
